Just now

Generated by Michael Todasco using ChatGPT Image Generation on July 12, 2026Disclaimer: This article describes an experiment and is NOT investment advice. And God forbid if you, too, are crazy enough to do an experiment like this yourself, only do it with money you plan to lose.
Predicting the Future
Oh, the power to see the future. In Avengers: Infinity War, Dr. Strange uses the magical time stones to see 14,000,605 potential outcomes of the upcoming conflict. And it is only one of those futures where the heroes are victorious. (Spoiler: they happen to be in the one timeline where they win…)
Wanting to predict and understand the future is a natural, recurring part of human existence. Whether they were patterns we saw in the sun and the stars, or Infinity Stones in a Marvel movie, or AI today.
I’m slightly obsessed with people who claim to predict the future. I recently vibe-coded Forecast Fools, which measures how well AI researchers and pundits predict AI progress (Spoiler: not very). AIs themselves have gotten extremely good at predicting things like the weather. But can an off-the-shelf AI “predict” something even more complex? Something with measurable outcomes and real stakes. Can I do with a $20 subscription what might take millions of dollars of algorithms and highly incentivized quants? To do that, I’d better call Sol.
OpenAI’s New 5.6 Models
Last week, OpenAI released their latest batch of models and, for some reason, gave them a brand-new nomenclature. They’re all ChatGPT 5.6, but within that naming, they added Luna (fast/lower-end), Terra (mid-range), and Sol (the all-powerful Infinity stones). Got that?
The models seem great. They’re powerful; they can do most any task I give them. But can Sol predict the future?
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Generated by Michael Todasco using ChatGPT Image Generation on July 14, 2026The Test
Back in May, Robinhood announced they were allowing their customers to set up separate “agentic” accounts that would tie into Claude, ChatGPT, or Cursor. It would be separate from your other accounts (to effectively limit your downside should the agents go rogue) and, once set up, run autonomously. In many ways, it is the perfect training ground for a predictive experiment. The stock market is possibly the purest adversarial, measurable prediction test we have. It is in the open and has real-time feedback to see if your predictions are holding true.
With that challenge, I gave the new 5.6 Sol model a $1000 Robinhood account, minimal constraints, and a “simple” task: “beat the S&P 500.”
That’s it. I didn’t say by how much. I didn’t say what it can/cannot do. Just beat the market. It can’t be that hard for the most powerful technology that humankind has ever created. Right?
Will it Beat the Market?
Full disclosure, I got my finance degree back in the 90s when the efficient market hypothesis was the law of the markets. In general, it states that you can’t beat the market over the long haul. My Vanguard-focused public investing over the last 25 years has largely reflected that mentality.
So, will my overpowered AI be able to choose tickers with magical properties to beat the market? Maybe. I think this is largely going to be a coin flip. And unfortunately, doing this with one instance of Sol will not produce anything of statistical significance. (Although doing it with a thousand different Sol instances might- so if anyone has $1M they want to commit to having 1,000 Sols invest their savings, I’m game.)
Tracking it
This is all going to be done in public. Once trades are placed, you’ll see it here:
https://almostmike.github.io/agentstocks/
On day one, Sol decided to buy Nvidia (which in itself is 7% of the S&P 500), NetApp, and Archer-Daniels-Midland (I initially thought Sol had made a mistake and purchased ADM instead of AMD). One day in, we’re whooping the S&P 500 by half a percentage point. Do that for the next 365 days, and we’re talking real money…
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Day one… losing but not as badly :)Fourteen million futures, yet Dr. Strange still only needed one. Sol’s got 365 days and a single timeline to work with. I don’t think this will be successful, but I’ve been wrong about AI before, and tracking this in public is how we’ll find out if I’m wrong again.
WDYT? Place your own bets in the comments.
